questions.prompt: Which tools should always be behind an approval gate?

choices.text: Tools with irreversible side effects: sending messages (email/SMS), moving money (charge/refund), deleting data, changing external state irreversibly (publish, cancel). Read-only tools and reversible internal actions don't need gates — gating everything creates friction without safety value.

questions.explanation: Approval gates earn their friction on irreversible side effects. Read-only tools, reversible internal actions, and the user's own draft work don't need them. The line is: 'could a wrong call cause harm we can't reverse?' If yes, gate it. If no, don't.

questions.prompt: Your team gates every refund for human review. Throughput suffers. Better pattern?

choices.text: Add a rule engine that auto-approves low-risk refunds (small amounts + customers in good standing) and routes only ambiguous or high-risk cases to humans. Layer with post-hoc audit on the auto-approved bucket so a human reviews periodically. Three-way routing: auto-approve, escalate, auto-reject by rules.
Require two-person approval to slow it down further.

questions.explanation: Three-way routing keeps human pressure on the cases that need it. Auto-approve the clear-cut safe ones; auto-reject the clear-cut risky ones; escalate the middle. Combine with audit logging so even auto-approvals are auditable post-hoc. Captures most of the safety value with most of the throughput.
